preamble: The transformation of the close front unrounded vowel /i/ to the nasalized close-mid front unrounded vowel /ẽ/ is a well-attested historical phonetic shift. This change typically involves two primary processes: nasalization, often triggered by a neighboring nasal consonant, and subsequent vowel lowering. The nasalization causes the velum to lower, allowing air to escape through the nose, while the vowel lowering shifts the tongue position from a high to a close-mid position in the mouth.
phoneticEffects: Vowel lowering, nasalization, compensatory nasalization (often following the loss of a nasal consonant)

examples.note: In the history of French, oral vowels followed by nasal consonants became nasalized. High nasal vowels, such as [ĩ], subsequently lowered to mid-nasal vowels like [ɛ̃] or [œ̃] between the 13th and 18th centuries.
In the Horb dialect of Swabian, oral /i/ undergoes regressive nasalization and surfaces as [ẽ].

sources: Kennedy Terry, K., & Russell, E. (n.d.). Modeling the emergence of a typological anomaly: Vowel nasalization in French. Proceedings.
Hajek, J. (n.d.). Investigating universals of sound change: the effect of vowel height and duration on the development of distinctive nasalization - Minerva Access.
Kniess, T. B. (n.d.). NASALIZATION AND NASAL VOWELS IN THE SWABIAN DIALECT OF HORB: A PHONOLOGICAL ANALYSIS OF KAUFFMANN 1890. IU ScholarWorks.
